cs_pp_019 2025 Batch 24 What will be the value of the following Python expression? (~0b0101 & 0o17) ^ (0xE >> 2) Step 1: Convert literals.
0b0101 = 5, 0o17 = 15 (octal 17 = 1×8+7=15), 0xE = 14.

Step 2: ~0b0101 = ~5 = −6 (bitwise NOT in Python flips all bits of the two's-complement representation: ~n = −n−1).

Step 3: (−6) & 15.
−6 in binary (two's complement) = …11111010. 15 = 00001111. AND gives 00001010 = 10.

Step 4: 0xE >> 2 = 14 >> 2 = 3 (right shift by 2 divides by 4 integer division).

Step 5: 10 ^ 3.
10 = 1010, 3 = 0011. XOR = 1001 = 9

cs_pp_042 2025 Batch 24 Consider the following Python code. x = 0.3 - 0.2 y = 0.2 - 0.1 print(x == y) When it is executed, the output is "False". What would be the reason? Floating-point numbers are stored in binary (IEEE 754). Decimal fractions like 0.1, 0.2, and 0.3 cannot be represented exactly in binary; they are stored as the closest approximation. As a result, 0.3 - 0.2 and 0.2 - 0.1 accumulate different rounding errors and produce slightly different binary values — even though mathematically both equal 0.1.

The == operator compares the actual stored values, which differ. This is the well-known floating-point precision problem. Option (d) i

questions.searchText: cs_pp_697 2020 Batch 19 What will be the output of the following Python code? i = 90 while (i < 100): j = 2 while (j <= (i/j)): if not(i%j): break j += 1 if (j > i/j) : print(i) i += 1 This code prints prime numbers between 90 and 99 inclusive. The inner loop checks whether any integer j from 2 up to √i divides i. If no divisor is found (j exceeds √i), the number is prime and is printed. Checking each: 90=2×45, 91=7×13, 92=4×23, 93=3×31, 94=2×47, 95=5×19, 96=2×48, 97 is prime (no divisors up to √97≈9.8), 98=2×49, 99=9×11. Only 97 is prime in [90,99]. The marking scheme confirms: answer 37 = 97. None 91 and 97 97 97 and 99

The system head curve is H_sys = 50 + h_f, where h_f = λ(L/D)(V²/2g) = λ(L/D)(Q²/(A²×2g)).

Using the Darcy equation: h_f = λ × (L/D) × Q²/(A² × 2g).
A = π(0.3)²/4 = 0.07069 m², A² = 0.004997 m⁴.
h_f = 0.0275 × (950/0.3) × Q²/(0.004997 × 2 × 9.81)
= 0.0275 × 3166.67 × Q²/0.09807
= 87.083 × Q²/0.09807
= 887.96 Q² ≈ 888 Q².

The most electropositive element: P has configuration ending in 3s¹ — a single valence electron (like sodium/Na). It very easily loses that one electron, making it the most electropositive. Electropositive character increases as valence electrons decrease and are more easily donated. P Q R S T

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_020 1 2012 📋 Reference data for Q20–Q23 (2012) Electron configurations: P — …2s²2p⁶ 3s¹ Q — …2s²2p⁶ 3s²3p⁶ 3d⁶4s² R — …2s²2p⁶ 3s²3p⁶ 3d¹⁰4s²4p⁵ S — …2s²2p⁶ 3s²3p² T — …2s²2p⁶ 3s²3p⁶ From configs above (P–T 2012 set), element(s) that can form covalent bonds with S (3s²3p²): S has 4 valence electrons (like Si) and can share electrons covalently. R (3d¹⁰4s²4p⁵) is halogen-like and can share electrons with S. S can also form covalent bonds with itself. So R and S can both form covalent bonds with S. R & S Q R S T

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_021 1 2012 📋 Reference data for Q20–Q23 (2012) Electron configurations: P — …2s²2p⁶ 3s¹ Q — …2s²2p⁶ 3s²3p⁶ 3d⁶4s² R — …2s²2p⁶ 3s²3p⁶ 3d¹⁰4s²4p⁵ S — …2s²2p⁶ 3s²3p² T — …2s²2p⁶ 3s²3p⁶ From configs above (P–T 2012 set), element that usually forms van der Waals bonds within its atoms: T has configuration 3s²3p⁶ — a complete outer shell (noble gas configuration, like Argon). Noble gases interact only through van der Waals forces. P Q R S T

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_022 1 2012 📋 Reference data for Q20–Q23 (2012) Electron configurations: P — …2s²2p⁶ 3s¹ Q — …2s²2p⁶ 3s²3p⁶ 3d⁶4s² R — …2s²2p⁶ 3s²3p⁶ 3d¹⁰4s²4p⁵ S — …2s²2p⁶ 3s²3p² T — …2s²2p⁶ 3s²3p⁶ From configs above (P–T 2012 set), the metal that can be used in high-strength applications: Q has configuration …3d⁶4s² — a transition metal (like iron) with a partially filled d shell. The mixed metallic-covalent d-orbital bonding gives transition metals exceptional strength for high-performance applications. P Q R S T

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_023 1 2012 The statement(s) NOT included in Bohr theory: Bohr theory states (i) and (iii). The Pauli Exclusion Principle (ii) and the Aufbau Principle (iv) are quantum mechanical rules that came after Bohr's model. Both (ii) and (iv) are NOT part of Bohr's original theory. Electrons are restricted to specific energy levels No two electrons can have all four quantum numbers the same Electrons move from lower to higher energy levels when receiving energy Sub-levels with lowest energy are filled first Both (ii) and (iv)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_024 1 2012 Which of the following bonds are NOT non-directional? (i.e., which is directional) Ionic, metallic, and van der Waals bonds are non-directional — they act equally in all directions. Covalent bonds are directional because they involve specific orbital overlaps at defined angles (bond angles determined by electron geometry). Ionic bond Covalent bond Metallic bond Van der Waals bond Both (i) and (ii)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_025 1 2005 High carbon steels are: Increasing carbon content in steel increases hardness through solid solution and carbide formation, but also increases brittleness by hindering dislocation movement. High carbon steel is therefore hard and brittle. Hard and tough Hard and brittle Tough and ductile Soft and brittle

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_026 1 2005 The type(s) of bonding available in polymers: Polymer chains are held together internally by strong covalent bonds (C–C backbone). Between different polymer chains, weak van der Waals (and sometimes hydrogen) bonds act. So polymers have covalent and/or van der Waals bonding. Covalent only Ionic only Covalent and/or van der Waals Ionic and/or van der Waals

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_027 1 2005 Ceramic materials are: Ceramics are characterized by strong ionic and/or covalent bonds, making them very hard. However, these bonds resist plastic deformation (dislocation movement is very difficult), making ceramics brittle. They are hard and brittle. Hard and tough Strong and ductile Soft and brittle Hard and brittle

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_028 1 2018 Which combination of quantum numbers is NOT available for an electron? (i) n=2, l=0, ml=0, ms=-1/2 (ii) n=2, l=2, ml=1, ms=-1/2 (iii) n=3, l=2, ml=1, ms=+1/2 (iv) n=3, l=1, ml=0, ms=+1/2 (v) n=3, l=0, ml=0, ms=+1/2 Combination (ii) is invalid: the secondary quantum number l must satisfy 0 <= l <= n-1. For n=2, l can only be 0 or 1. l=2 when n=2 is impossible. All other combinations obey the quantum number rules. (i) (ii) (iii) (iv) (v)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_029 1 2013 Transition metals like tungsten and molybdenum have bonds which are: Transition metals exhibit primary metallic bonding (delocalised sea of electrons) but their partially filled d-orbitals also allow directional covalent bonding contributions. This hybrid accounts for their exceptionally high melting points and mechanical strength compared to s-block metals. Metallic only Covalent only Metallic and covalent Metallic and ionic Metallic and van der Waals

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_030 1 2012 Which of the following is TRUE? Silica glass is an amorphous ceramic with a continuous random network of strong covalent Si-O bonds, modified by network ions (Na+, Ca2+) that introduce ionic character. The others are false: Cu has a full 3d10 shell; diamond is far stiffer than iron; amorphous metallic glasses do exist. Cu is a transition metal because its d-orbital is not fully occupied Intermolecular bonds always decide the melting point and strength of a composite Glass contains ionic and covalent bonds though it is an amorphous ceramic Iron has a larger elastic modulus compared with diamond All metals are crystalline

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_031 1 2012 All possible magnetic quantum numbers for an electron on the second main energy level (n=2) are: For n=2, l can be 0 or 1. The magnetic quantum number ml ranges from -l to +l. For l=0: ml=0. For l=1: ml = -1, 0, +1. The complete set of all possible ml values at n=2 is {-1, 0, +1}. -2, -1, 0, 1, 2 -1, 0, 1 0 only -1, 1 -2, -1, 1, 2

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_032 1 2010 Reference for Q28-Q31 (2010): Figure 1 periodic table. P=s-block (far left, electropositive metal). Q=d-block (transition metal). R=p-block right (metalloid/non-metal). S=Group 17 (halogen, highly electronegative). T=Group 18 (noble gas). Using Figure 1, which element can form an ionic bond with element P? P is an s-block electropositive metal that readily loses electrons. For an ionic bond it needs a highly electronegative non-metal to accept those electrons. S is in Group 17 (halogens) - the most electronegative group that forms stable anions, making S the ideal ionic partner for P. P Q R S T

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_033 1 2010 Reference for Q28-Q31 (2010): Figure 1 periodic table. P=s-block (far left, electropositive metal). Q=d-block (transition metal). R=p-block right (metalloid/non-metal). S=Group 17 (halogen, highly electronegative). T=Group 18 (noble gas). Using Figure 1, which element(s) can form covalent bonds with element R? R is in the middle p-block (like C or Si). It can share electrons with itself (R) to form covalent network solids, and it can share electrons with halogens (S) to form covalent molecules. Both R and S are electronegative enough to share rather than fully transfer electrons. R & S Q R only S only T

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_034 1 2010 Reference for Q28-Q31 (2010): Figure 1 periodic table. P=s-block (far left, electropositive metal). Q=d-block (transition metal). R=p-block right (metalloid/non-metal). S=Group 17 (halogen, highly electronegative). T=Group 18 (noble gas). Using Figure 1, which element usually forms van der Waals bonds between its atoms? T is in Group 18 (noble gases). With a completely full outer shell it has essentially zero tendency to form primary chemical bonds. The only interactions between its atoms are weak London dispersion (van der Waals) forces. P Q R S T

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_035 1 2010 Reference for Q28-Q31 (2010): Figure 1 periodic table. P=s-block (far left, electropositive metal). Q=d-block (transition metal). R=p-block right (metalloid/non-metal). S=Group 17 (halogen, highly electronegative). T=Group 18 (noble gas). Using Figure 1, which metal can be used in high-strength applications? Q is in the d-block (transition metals). Transition metals use both s and partially filled d-orbital electrons for bonding, producing a hybrid of metallic and covalent bonding that grants superior strength, hardness, and high-temperature performance. P Q R S T

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_036 1 2010 Which of the following bonds are directional? Covalent bonds are highly directional because they result from the overlap of specific electron orbitals, dictating precise bond angles and molecular geometry. Ionic, metallic, and van der Waals bonds exert forces equally in all directions (non-directional). Ionic bond Covalent bond Metallic bond Van der Waals bond Both (i) and (ii)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_037 1 2009 Which of the following is correct according to Bohr's theory? The core postulate of Bohr's model is that electrons orbit only in discrete, quantised energy levels. Option (ii) is the Pauli Exclusion Principle; option (iv) is the Aufbau Principle — both are modern quantum mechanics rules, not Bohr's original theory. Electrons of an atom are restricted to specific energy levels No two electrons in a single atom can have all four quantum numbers the same When an electron receives energy it moves from a lower to a higher energy level Sub-levels with the lowest energy are filled before those with higher energy

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_038 1 2009 If the principal quantum number is 3, how many possible values are there for the secondary quantum number? The secondary (azimuthal) quantum number l ranges from 0 to n-1. For n=3, possible values are l=0, 1, 2 — exactly 3 values corresponding to the s, p, and d subshells. 2 3 4 5

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_039 1 2009 Which of the following is/are non-directional bonds? Ionic bonds act spherically between charged ions (non-directional). Van der Waals forces are also non-directional. Covalent bonds require specific orbital overlap (directional), and hydrogen bonds depend on precise linear dipole alignment (also directional). Hydrogen bond Covalent bond Ionic bond Van der Waals bond Both (iii) and (iv)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_040 1 2009 Transition metals are: Transition metals are d-block elements (progressively filling d-orbitals). Their partial d-orbital covalent bonding contribution on top of metallic bonding makes them generally stronger, harder, and higher melting than s-block metals. p-block elements d-block elements Non-metals Stronger than other metals Both (ii) and (iv)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_041 1 2005 The relative atomic mass of metal X is 60. What is the mass of one atom of X? (Avogadro's number = 6.022 x 10^23 mol^-1) One mole of X has a mass of 60 g. Mass of one atom = molar mass / Avogadro's number = 60 / (6.022 x 10^23) = 9.96 x 10^-23 g per atom. 60 kg 60 g 9.96 x 10^-23 kg 9.96 x 10^-23 g

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_042 1 2005 Which of the following is NOT a non-directional bond? The question asks which IS directional. Metallic, ionic, and van der Waals bonds are all non-directional. Hydrogen bonds are directional — their strength depends on near-linear alignment of the hydrogen atom between two electronegative atoms. Metallic Hydrogen Ionic Van der Waals

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_043 1 2005 Which of the following is NOT a composite material? A composite combines two or more distinct materials. GFRP (glass fibres in polymer matrix), asphalt mix (bitumen + aggregate), and chip board (wood chips + resin) are all composites. Cement itself is a single-phase ceramic binder — it only becomes a composite when combined with aggregates to form concrete. GFRP Asphalt mix Cement Chip board

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_044 1 2005 Stainless steel is: Stainless steel is a ferrous (iron-based) alloy steel, distinguished from plain carbon steel by significant alloying additions — at minimum 10.5% chromium — which forms a passive oxide layer giving corrosion resistance. A non-ferrous alloy A plain carbon steel An alloy steel An amorphous metal

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_045 1 2005 "Aluminium is a ceramic material." State whether this is TRUE or FALSE. Aluminium is a pure metal: metallic bonding, high electrical and thermal conductivity, plastic malleability. Ceramics are inorganic non-metallic solids (like aluminium oxide) with ionic/covalent bonds. The statement is FALSE. TRUE FALSE

questions.searchText: materials_pu_046 1 2002 Covalent and ionic solids are not suitable for constructional purposes because they: Although covalent and ionic solids have very strong bonds, their rigid bond structures prevent plastic deformation. Unlike metals where dislocations absorb energy, these materials cannot deform plastically and instead fracture catastrophically — they are brittle. Have weak bonds Are brittle Are ductile Are expensive

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_047 2 2010 The relative atomic mass of Po is 210. It crystallizes in simple cubic structure with density 9.32 g/cm³. Find the lattice constant of Po. (Avogadro's number = 6.023×10²³ mol⁻¹) For SC: 1 atom per unit cell. ρ = nM/(Nₐ·a³) → a³ = 210/(6.023×10²³ × 9.32) = 3.745×10⁻²³ cm³. a = (3.745×10⁻²³)^(1/3) ≈ 3.35×10⁻⁸ cm = 3.34 Å. 4.21 Å 5.31 Å 3.34 Å 421.4 Å 334.45 Å

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_048 2 2010 What is the radius of the largest impurity atom that can be placed within the void space at the body-center of an FCC lattice without distorting the arrangement? The octahedral void at the body-center of FCC has a radius ratio r/R = 0.414 ≈ 0.4. So the largest impurity atom that fits without distortion has radius ≈ 0.4R, where R is the parent atom radius. Equal to the radius of parent atoms 0.4 times the radius of parent atoms 0.7 times the radius of parent atoms 0.8 times the radius of parent atoms 1.5 times the radius of parent atoms

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_049 2 2010 What will be the packing factor of a simple cubic lattice when all the void spaces are occupied by foreign atoms that just fit into those voids? SC packing factor alone is 0.524. The cubic void at body center of SC has r/R = 0.732. Adding those foreign atoms (which touch the corner atoms) increases the total volume fraction. The combined packing factor comes to approximately 0.561. 0.524 0.561 0.73 0.74 0.68

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_050 2 2010 In a FCC unit cell (lattice parameter a), X is the midpoint of edge UV (so UX=XV). What are the Miller Indices of direction OX? If O is the origin (0,0,0) and X is the midpoint of an edge like U(0,1,1) to V(1,1,1), X is located at (1/2, 1, 1). The direction vector OX is [1/2 1 1]. To clear the fractions, multiply by 2 to get the Miller indices [1 2 2]. Square brackets denote a direction. [1/2 1 1] [2 1 1] (2 1 1) [1 2 2] (1 2 2)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_051 2 2013 A metal has atomic mass 26, cubic structure with lattice parameter 4.0 Å, and density 2.7 g/cm³. Identify its structure. (Avogadro = 6.023×10²³) n = ρ·Nₐ·a³/M = (2.7 × 6.023×10²³ × (4×10⁻⁸)³)/26 = (2.7 × 6.023×10²³ × 6.4×10⁻²³)/26 ≈ 4 atoms/unit cell. A cubic structure with 4 atoms/cell is FCC (face-centred cubic). Simple cubic BCC FCC HCP BCT

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_052 2 2013 Miller indices of a plane intersecting x, y, z axes at a, a/2, a/3 respectively (a = lattice parameter): Intercepts: 1, 1/2, 1/3. Take reciprocals: 1, 2, 3. Clear fractions (already integers). Miller indices = (1 2 3). Note: plane indices use parentheses (). (1 1/2 1/3) [1 1/2 1/3] (1 2 3) [1 2 3] (6 3 2)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_053 2 2013 What volume percentage of an FCC lattice will be void when half the octahedral interstices are occupied by foreign atoms that just fit in? FCC packing factor is 0.74, meaning 26% is void. An octahedral void has radius r = 0.414R. The volume of one foreign atom is (0.414)³ ≈ 0.071 times a host atom. Filling 2 of the 4 voids adds ~2.6% to the occupied volume (74% + 2.6% = 76.6%). This leaves 100% - 76.6% = 23.4%, or about 23% void space. 74% 26% 79% 21% 23%

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_054 2 2013 The ratio of the radius of an octahedral interstice to the radius of the parent atom in an FCC lattice is: For an octahedral void in FCC, the geometry gives r_void/R_atom = 0.414. 0.414 0.523 0.740 0.812 0.828

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_055 2 2013 What is the planar density of (110) plane of FCC in terms of atomic radius R? FCC (110) plane has atoms at 4 corners (each 1/4) + 2 face centers on the long edges (each 1/2) = 1+1 = 2 atoms. Area = a × a√2 = a²√2. In FCC, a = 2R√2. Area = (2R√2)²×√2 = 8R²√2 ≈ 11.31R². Planar density = 2/(11.31R²) ≈ 0.177 R⁻². 0.088 R⁻² 0.088 R² 0.177 R⁻² 0.177 R² 11.3 R⁻²

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_056 2 2014 Miller indices of a plane intersecting x, y, z axes at 0.25a, 0.5a, 0.75a (a = lattice parameter): Intercepts: 1/4, 1/2, 3/4 (in units of a). Reciprocals: 4, 2, 4/3. Multiply by 3 to clear fractions: 12, 6, 4. Miller indices = (12 6 4). Parentheses for planes. (1/4 1/2 3/4) (4 2 4/3) (12, 6, 4) (12 6 4) [12 6 4]

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_057 2 2014 Majority of the polymers are __________ in nature. Most polymers are semi-crystalline — they have both crystalline regions (where polymer chains are regularly ordered) and amorphous regions (randomly coiled chains). Very few polymers are fully crystalline; many technical polymers are semi-crystalline. Crystalline Non-crystalline Semi-crystalline Amorphous Both (ii) and (iv)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_058 2 2014 What volume percentage of a hexagonal close packed (HCP) structure will be usually void? HCP has the same packing factor as FCC (both are close-packed structures): 74% occupied, 26% void. The arrangement of close-packed layers (ABABAB in HCP vs ABCABC in FCC) achieves the same maximum packing density. 74% 26% 68% 32% 23%

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_059 2 2014 A metal changes from FCC to simple cubic on cooling. What happens to the volume if atomic radius change is negligible? FCC packing factor = 0.74; SC packing factor = 0.52. If the atoms stay the same size but rearrange from FCC to SC, they pack less efficiently. To maintain the same number of atoms, the volume must increase. FCC→SC transition increases volume. Volume decreases on cooling Volume increases on cooling Volume remains the same Volume change is negligible None of the above is true

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_060 2 2014 Close packed direction(s) in a BCC structure: In BCC, atoms touch along the body diagonal. The body diagonal direction is [111]. Along [100] and [110], atoms do NOT touch. Therefore [111] is the only close packed direction in BCC. [100] [110] [111] Both [100] and [110] Both [110] and [111]

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_061 2 2014 Close packed direction(s) in an FCC structure: In FCC, atoms touch along the face diagonal. The face diagonal direction is [110]. Along [100], atoms do NOT touch (gap between them). Along [111] (body diagonal) in FCC, atoms also do not touch. Therefore [110] is the close packed direction in FCC. [100] [110] [111] Both [100] and [110] Both [110] and [111]

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_062 2 2014 Close packed plane(s) in a BCC structure: BCC structures do not have any true close-packed planes (where the planar packing fraction is 0.906). While the (110) plane is the *most* densely packed plane within the BCC system, it does not meet the strict crystallographic definition of a close-packed plane. (100) (110) (111) Both (100) and (110) None of the above

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_063 2 2014 Close packed plane(s) in an FCC structure: In FCC, the (111) plane is the close-packed plane — atoms touch along [110] directions within this plane and achieve the maximum planar packing density. FCC and HCP share this (111) close-packed plane characteristic. (100) (110) (111) Both (100) and (110) None of the above

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_064 2 2015 An interstitial is: An interstitial (self-interstitial or interstitial impurity) is a point defect where an atom occupies a position that is not a normal lattice site — i.e., it sits in the space between regular lattice positions. A defect involving several atoms over a finite volume A pair of point defects when an ion moves leaving a vacancy (Frenkel) A point defect when an atom is placed at a site not normally a lattice point A defect from an atom leaving a vacant lattice site (vacancy) An interface separating two crystal groups of different crystallographic orientation

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_065 2 2015 A grain boundary is: A grain boundary is a planar (2D) defect — specifically an interface that separates two grains (crystals) having different crystallographic orientations. It is classified as a surface/interfacial defect, not a point or line defect. A defect involving several atoms over a finite volume A Frenkel pair defect A point defect at a non-lattice site A vacancy defect An interface separating two crystal groups of different crystallographic orientations

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_066 2 2015 Which plane has the greatest planar density in FCC? In FCC, the (111) plane is the most densely packed — 2 atoms per unit cell area of a²√3/2, giving planar density = 4/(√3·a²) = 0.2296/R². This is higher than (100) (0.159/R²) or (110) (0.112/R²). (100) (110) (111) (100) & (110) (110) & (111)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_067 2 2015 Which plane has the greatest planar density in BCC? In BCC, the (110) plane has the highest planar density. It contains 2 atoms per a²√2 area. This is because the close-packed direction [111] lies within the (110) plane, so atoms are touching within this plane. (100) (110) (111) (100) & (110) (110) & (111)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_068 2 2015 What volume percentage of a BCC lattice will be usually void? BCC has an atomic packing factor (APF) of 0.68, meaning 68% of the unit cell volume is occupied by atoms. The remaining 32% is void space. 74% 26% 68% 32% 23%

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_069 2 2015 In simple cubic structure, nearest neighbors are at distance a. What is the nearest neighbor distance in BCC? In BCC, atoms touch along the body diagonal [111]. The body diagonal length = a√3. Each body diagonal connects two corner atoms through the body center, so nearest neighbor distance = (a√3)/2 ≈ 0.866a ≈ 0.9a (exam approximation). More precisely ≈0.866a. a 0.5a 0.7a 0.8a 0.9a

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_070 2 2015 What is the nearest neighbor distance in FCC structure? Wait — in FCC, atoms touch along the face diagonal [110]. Face diagonal = a√2. Nearest neighbor distance = a√2/2 = 0.707a ≈ 0.7a. Same numerical answer as BCC but for a different direction. a 0.5a 0.7a 0.8a 0.9a

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_071 2 2017 A group of crystals having a particular crystallographic orientation different from its neighbors is known as: A grain is defined as a region (group of unit cells) all having the same crystallographic orientation. Different grains are separated by grain boundaries. A polycrystalline material consists of many grains. An edge dislocation A screw dislocation A grain A grain boundary A polycrystalline

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_072 2 2017 What is the shortest distance between two atoms of a close-packed cubic lattice of lattice parameter a? A close-packed cubic lattice is FCC. Atoms touch along the face diagonal: 4R = a√2, so 2R = a√2/2 = a/√2. The shortest distance between atom centers = 2R = a/√2. a a√2 a√3 a/√2 a√3/2

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_073 2 2017 Which of the following planes is/are close packed? Strictly speaking, only the (111) planes in FCC and the basal planes in HCP are true close-packed planes (with an atomic packing fraction of 0.906). The (110) plane in BCC is its *most densely* packed plane, but it is not a true close-packed plane because the atoms do not achieve maximum 2D packing. Therefore, none of the options are true close-packed planes. (100) of Simple Cubic (110) of BCC (111) of BCC (110) of FCC None of the above

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_074 2 2017 Which of the following directions is/are close packed? [110] of FCC: atoms touch along the face diagonal — YES, close packed. [110] of BCC: atoms do NOT touch along this direction — NO. [100] of BCC: not close packed. [110] of SC: atoms don't touch. Therefore only [110] of FCC is close packed among these options. [110] of Simple Cubic [110] of BCC [100] of BCC [110] of FCC All except (iii)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_075 2 2017 Iron changes from BCC to FCC structure on heating above 910°C. Such behavior of iron is called: When a solid element can exist in more than one crystal structure depending on temperature or pressure, it is called allotropy (or polymorphism for compounds). Iron's α (BCC) to γ (FCC) transition at 910°C is a classic example of allotropy. Allotropy Melting Polymeric Extraction Both (i) and (iii) are correct

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_076 2 2017 What happens to the volume of iron during the BCC→FCC transition on heating above 910°C? The volume change depends on both the change in atomic radius and packing efficiency. BCC has APF = 0.68 and FCC has APF = 0.74. If the atomic radius were unchanged, the denser FCC packing would reduce volume. However, the atomic radius itself changes during the BCC→FCC transition — and if the radius increases enough, the volume can actually increase despite the higher packing efficiency. For example, if the radius grows from a smaller BCC value to a noticeably larger FCC value, the extra space each atom occupies can outweigh the gain in packing efficiency, resulting in a net volu

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_077 2 2017 An FCC lattice and a BCC lattice have the same lattice parameter. Which quantity will also be the same? If both lattices have the same lattice parameter a, the unit cell volume = a³ is identical for both. However, atomic radius, packing factor, and number of atoms per unit cell are all different for FCC (4 atoms, APF=0.74, R=a√2/4) vs BCC (2 atoms, APF=0.68, R=a√3/4). Atomic radius Atomic packing factor Number of atoms per unit cell Volume of a single unit cell None of the above

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_078 2 2018 In a BCC unit cell, the body-center atom has 8 nearest neighbours (the corner atoms). How many nearest neighbours does a corner atom have? In BCC, each corner atom is shared by 8 unit cells. Each unit cell has one body-center atom that is a nearest neighbor. So each corner atom has nearest neighbors from 8 surrounding unit cells → 8 nearest neighbors. BCC coordination number = 8. One Six Eight Ten Twelve

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_079 2 2018 The number of atoms per unit cell for a BCC structure: BCC unit cell: 8 corner atoms × 1/8 each = 1, plus 1 body-center atom × 1 = 1. Total = 2 atoms per unit cell. Nine Eight Six Five Two

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_080 2 2018 What is the fraction of the volume occupied by atoms in a BCC unit cell? The atomic packing factor (APF) for BCC = (2 × (4/3)πR³)/a³. In BCC, R = a√3/4. Substituting: APF = 2 × (4/3)π × (a√3/4)³/a³ = π√3/8 ≈ 0.680. So 68% of the volume is occupied by atoms. 0.52 0.26 0.32 0.68 0.74

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_081 2 2018 What is the shortest distance between two atoms of a close-packed cubic lattice of lattice parameter a? FCC is the close-packed cubic lattice. Atoms touch along [110] (face diagonal = a√2). Nearest neighbor distance = 2R = a√2/2 = a/√2. a a√2 a√3 a√2/2 a√3/2

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_082 2 2018 How many octahedral interstices are there in an FCC unit cell? FCC has 4 atoms per unit cell and 4 octahedral interstices per unit cell: 1 body-center site + 12 edge-center sites × 1/4 each = 1 + 3 = 4. So there are 4 octahedral interstices per FCC unit cell. 3 4 6 12 13

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_083 2 2010 Figure 2 shows an FCC unit cell with lattice parameter a. If UX = XV, find the planar density of the plane parallel to OSVR passing through X. The plane through X parallel to OSVR is a (200) mid-plane of the FCC cell. It intersects the centres of exactly 4 face-centred atoms (on the 4 surrounding faces). Each contributes ¼ of its area to the a×a square cross-section, giving a total of 1 equivalent atom. Planar density = 1/a² = a⁻². a⁻² 2a⁻² 4a⁻² a⁻²/√2 √2 a⁻²

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_084 2 2010 📋 Reference for Q5 (2010) The (200) mid-plane of FCC contains 4 face-centred atoms arranged at the corners of a square. These atoms touch each other along the face diagonals. Which diagram correctly shows the atomic arrangement of the plane from Q4? The 4 face-centred atoms on the (200) cross-section touch each other along the face diagonals of the perpendicular faces. This creates 4 equal circles arranged at the corners of a square, each touching its two neighbours — exactly as shown in diagram (ii). Diagram (i) Diagram (ii) Diagram (iii) Diagram (iv) Diagram (v)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_085 2 2010 In Figure 2, what are the Miller indices of the plane SXR? From the geometry of Figure 2 the plane SXR intercepts the x-axis at a, the y-axis at a/2, and the z-axis at a/2. Taking reciprocals: 1, 2, 2. Parentheses denote a plane, giving (122). Square brackets would denote a direction. (1/2 1 1) [211] (211) [122] (122)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_086 2 2010 Which of the following is NOT a crystalline defect? A grain is a continuous, perfectly ordered single-crystal region within a polycrystalline material. It is the fundamental ordered structure — not a defect. The grain boundary (2D defect), dislocations (1D line defects), and self-interstitial atoms (0D point defects) are all classified as crystalline defects. Grain Grain boundary Dislocations Self-interstitial atom None of the above

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_087 2 2013 Which defect(s) form during the solidification process? All three form naturally during solidification: (i) As multiple crystal nuclei grow and meet, their misoriented boundaries become grain boundaries. (ii) When crystal growth fronts collide with slight misalignment, dislocations are created. (iii) At any temperature above 0 K, thermal fluctuations leave some lattice sites vacant. All three are unavoidable consequences of solidification. Grain boundary Dislocations Vacancy All of the above None of the above

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_088 2 2013 Which of the following planes passes through the body centre of a BCC unit cell? The (110) plane is a diagonal plane that cuts through two opposite vertical edges of the cube and passes directly through the geometric body-centre. In BCC, this plane contains 2 corner atoms (4 × ¼ shared = 1) plus the body-centre atom, giving the 4-circle touching arrangement shown in diagram (ii). Diagram (ii) is the (110) plane of BCC. Diagram (i) Diagram (ii) Diagram (iii) Diagram (iv) Diagram (v)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_089 2 2014 Which of the following is/are usually amorphous at room temperature? Glass is the textbook example of an amorphous solid — it has no long-range crystalline order. Liquid water also lacks long-range crystalline order and is amorphous. Diamond is a perfectly crystalline covalent solid with a well-defined crystal structure. Therefore only water and glass qualify. Water Glass Diamond All of the above Only (i) and (ii)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_090 2 2014 Which of the following planes is/are close packed? Close-packed means atoms physically touch along every direction within the plane. Diagram (i) resembles SC(100) where corner atoms do NOT touch (gap between them). Diagram (ii) looks like (110) of BCC where atoms touch in one direction but not the other. Diagram (iii) shows atoms only at corners with large gaps. None of these planes satisfies the strict close-packed criterion. Diagram (i) Diagram (ii) Diagram (iii) All of the above are close packed None of the above are close packed

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_091 2 2015 What are the Miller indices of a plane intersecting x, y, z at 0.25a, 0.5a, 0.75a? Take reciprocals of the intercepts in units of a: 1/0.25 = 4, 1/0.5 = 2, 1/0.75 = 4/3. To clear fractions multiply all by 3: 12, 6, 4. Miller indices of a plane are always enclosed in parentheses (not square brackets), giving (12 6 4). Option (iii) uses commas and is non-standard notation. (1/4, 1/2, 3/4) (4, 2, 4/3) (12, 6, 4) (12 6 4) [12 6 4]

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_092 2 2015 Which of the following planes is close packed? A close-packed plane requires atoms to touch in ALL directions within it. SC(100): corner atoms do not touch (a > 2R for SC). BCC(110): atoms touch along [111] body diagonal direction but NOT along the [001] edge direction. BCC(111): only 2 atoms and they do not touch. FCC(110): atoms touch along [110] but NOT along [001]. The truly close-packed plane in FCC is (111) — but that option is not listed. Therefore none of these are close packed. (100) of simple cubic (110) of BCC (111) of BCC (110) of FCC None of the above is close packed

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_093 2 2015 Which of the following directions is close packed? In simple cubic, a = 2R, so nearest-neighbour atoms sit at distance a = 2R along [100] — meaning they are touching. Therefore [100] of SC is a close-packed direction. BCC close-packed direction is [111] (body diagonal). FCC close-packed direction is [110] (face diagonal), not [111]. [100] of BCC has atoms that do not touch along the edge. [100] of simple cubic [110] of BCC [100] of BCC [111] of FCC None of the above is close packed

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_094 2 2017 What are the Miller indices of a plane intersecting x, y, z at 0.25a, 0.25a, 0.75a? Take reciprocals: 1/0.25 = 4, 1/0.25 = 4, 1/0.75 = 4/3. Multiply through by 3 to clear fractions: 12, 12, 4. Divide by common factor 4: 3, 3, 1. Enclose in parentheses for a plane: (3 3 1). (1/4 1/4 3/4) (4 4 4/3) (1 1 1/3) [12 12 4] (3 3 1)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_095 2 2017 What is the planar density of the (110) plane of BCC, if the atomic radius is R? The (110) plane of BCC contains 2 equivalent atoms (4 corner × ¼ + 1 centre = 2). The area of this rectangular plane is a × a√2. For BCC, a = 4R/√3, so a² = 16R²/3 and area = a²√2 = 16√2R²/3 ≈ 7.54R². Planar density = 2/7.54R² ≈ 1/3.77R² ≈ 1/3.8R². 1/3.8R² 1/4R² 1/5.7R² 1/7.5R² 1/11.3R²

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_096 2 2017 📋 Figure 1 Reference for Q40–Q42 (2017) All five points are on edges at distance a/3 from the nearest corner. Origin is at bottom-left-front. X-axis points toward viewer, Y points right, Z points up. In Figure 1, which point when connected to the origin shows direction [302]? Direction [302] has components x:y:z = 3:0:2. Normalize so the largest component hits the cell boundary: scale by a/3, giving (a, 0, 2a/3). This is a point on the XZ face at x = a, z = 2a/3, i.e. a/3 below the top-front edge. From the figure this is point (iv). Point (i) Point (ii) Point (iii) Point (iv) Point (v)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_097 2 2017 📋 Figure 1 Reference for Q40–Q42 (2017) All five points are on edges at distance a/3 from the nearest corner. Origin is at bottom-left-front. In Figure 1, which point when connected to the origin shows direction [233]? Direction [233] has components x:y:z = 2:3:3. Normalize so y and z = a: scale by a/3, giving (2a/3, a, a). This is the top-back-right edge corner displaced a/3 inward along x — point (ii) in Figure 1. Point (i) Point (ii) Point (iii) Point (iv) Point (v)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_098 2 2017 📋 Figure 1 Reference for Q40–Q42 (2017) All five points are on edges at distance a/3 from the nearest corner. Origin is at bottom-left-front. In Figure 1, which point when connected to the origin shows direction [023]? Direction [023] has x = 0, y:z = 2:3. Normalize so z = a: scale by a/3, giving (0, 2a/3, a). This lies on the top-back-left edge, displaced a/3 from the top-back corner along y — point (iii) in Figure 1. Point (i) Point (ii) Point (iii) Point (iv) Point (v)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_099 2 2018 📋 Figure 2 Reference for Q51 (2018) BCC-type structure: atom in the middle with 8 nearest neighbours at the corners. Side length a = 2 nm. Find the number of atoms per square metre of the (110) plane of the structure in Figure 2. Side length = 2 nm. Figure 2 is a BCC structure. The (110) plane contains 2 equivalent atoms (4 corners × ¼ + 1 body-centre = 2). Area = a × a√2 = 2×10⁻⁹ × 2√2×10⁻⁹ = 5.657×10⁻¹⁸ m². Planar density = 2 / 5.657×10⁻¹⁸ ≈ 3.54×10¹⁷ ≈ 3.5×10¹⁷ atoms/m². 2.5 × 10¹⁷ 5 × 10¹⁷ 3.5 × 10¹⁷ 1.8 × 10¹⁷ 2 × 10¹⁷

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_100 2 2018 📋 Figure 3 Reference for Q53–Q56 (2018) All five points are on edges at distance a/4 from the nearest corner. Origin is at bottom-left-front. X points toward viewer, Y points right, Z points up. In Figure 3, which point when connected to the origin shows direction [304]? Direction [304] has x:y:z = 3:0:4. Normalize so z = a: scale by a/4, giving (3a/4, 0, a). This point is on the top-front edge, a/4 from the top-front-left corner — point (iv) in Figure 3. Point (i) Point (ii) Point (iii) Point (iv) Point (v)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_101 2 2018 📋 Figure 3 Reference for Q53–Q56 (2018) All five points on edges at a/4 from nearest corner. In Figure 3, which point when connected to the origin shows direction [443]? Direction [443] has x:y:z = 4:4:3. Normalize so x and y = a: scale by a/4, giving (a, a, 3a/4). This is on the top-right-back edge, a/4 below the top corner — point (ii) in Figure 3. Point (i) Point (ii) Point (iii) Point (iv) Point (v)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_102 2 2018 📋 Figure 3 Reference for Q53–Q56 (2018) Point (ii) is located at approximately (a/4, 0, 3a/4) from the origin (on the left-front edge, a/4 from the top-front corner). In Figure 3, which point when connected to point (ii) shows direction [010]? Direction [010] points purely in the +Y direction. Starting from point (ii) on the left-front edge and moving only in +Y leads to the equivalent point on the right-front edge at the same height — point (v) in Figure 3, which shares the same x and z coordinates but is displaced by a in Y. Point (i) Point (ii) Point (iii) Point (iv) Point (v)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_103 2 2018 📋 Figure 3 Reference for Q53–Q56 (2018) Point (ii) is on the left-front edge at a/4 from the top corner. In Figure 3, which point when connected to point (ii) shows direction [110]? Direction [110] has equal +X and +Y components, zero Z. From point (ii) moving equally in +X and +Y (diagonally across the top face) leads to point (iv) — the point on the top-right-front edge at the same z-height as (ii). Point (i) Point (ii) Point (iii) Point (iv) Point (v)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_104 2 2018 📋 Figure 4 Reference for Q57–Q61 (2018) Cubic unit cell. O = origin (front-left-bottom). A = (a,0,0) along X. B = (0,a,0) along Y. C = far corner on Y-Z face. D = top-right-back. E = top-left (on Z axis). F = back-left-bottom. G = back-right-bottom. In Figure 4, what are the Miller indices of plane AEC? Plane AEC passes through three corners of the unit cell. Identifying their coordinates: A=(a,0,0), E=(0,0,a), C=(0,a,0) — each intercept is exactly a on one axis. Reciprocals: 1/1, 1/1, 1/1 = (111). This is the standard 111 close-packed plane of FCC. (111) (11̄1) (1̄11) (1̄11) (1̄1̄1)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_105 2 2018 📋 Figure 4 Reference for Q57–Q61 (2018) O = origin. A=(a,0,0), B=(0,a,0), C far on Y, D=top-right-back, E=(0,0,a), F=back-left, G=back-right. In Figure 4, what are the Miller indices of plane AGC? Plane AGC passes through A=(a,0,0), G=(0,a,0) on the bottom and C=(0,a,a) on the far edge. The intercepts are x=a (+1), y is parallel so ∞ in one sense — actually tracing the plane: it intersects x at +a and z at -a (negative z intercept) giving reciprocals (1, 0̄, 1) — more carefully the plane cuts x=+1, y=∞, z=-1 giving indices (11̄1). (111) (11̄1) (1̄11) (1̄1̄1) (1̄1̄1̄)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_106 2 2018 📋 Figure 4 Reference for Q57–Q61 (2018) Plane ABDE contains four labelled corners. In Figure 4, what are the Miller indices of plane ABDE? Plane ABDE contains points A=(a,0,0), B=(0,a,0), D=(0,a,a), E=(0,0,a). This plane intercepts x at +a and y at +a but is parallel to z (no z intercept → z intercept = ∞). Reciprocals: 1/1, 1/1, 1/∞ = 1, 1, 0. Miller indices = (110). (101) (110) (11̄0) (1̄10) (1̄01)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_107 2 2018 📋 Figure 4 Reference for Q60 (2018) BCC structure. Close-packed direction in BCC is [111] — the body diagonal. If Figure 4 is BCC, which direction is close packed? In BCC the close-packed direction is [111] — the body diagonal along which atoms touch: corner → body-centre → opposite corner. OD connects O=(0,0,0) to D=(a,a,a), which is exactly the [111] body diagonal. Therefore OD is the close-packed direction. OA BG AC BE OD

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_108 2 2018 📋 Figure 4 Reference for Q61 (2018) FCC structure. Close-packed direction in FCC is [110] — the face diagonal. If Figure 4 is FCC, which direction is close packed? In FCC the close-packed direction is [110] — the face diagonal along which atoms touch. EDB: E=(0,0,a), D=(a,a,a), B=(0,a,0). Vector ED = (a,a,0) which is [110], and vector DB = (-a,0,-a) = [-101]. These are two different directions so EDB as a path follows [110] then [-101] — but examining the path E→D→B, the segment E→D is [110] which IS the FCC close-packed direction. The correct answer is (iii) EDB following the [110] face diagonal. AFE EOB EDB OBD None of the above

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_109 3 2010 In which hardness testing method(s) is it required to measure the dimensions of the indentation? Vickers and Brinell hardness are both calculated from the size (area) of the indentation — you must measure the diagonal(s) of the impression under a microscope. Rockwell hardness is measured directly from the depth of penetration using a dial gauge, so no measurement of indentation dimensions is needed. Vickers only Rockwell only Brinell only All of the above Only Vickers and Brinell

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_110 3 2010 Indentation test can be used to: Indentation tests (Brinell, Vickers, Rockwell, Knoop) measure hardness — resistance to permanent indentation. They can be applied to any sufficiently stiff material including metals and wood. They do NOT directly measure toughness or strength (though hardness correlates with strength for metals). Measure the hardness of wood Measure the hardness of metals Determine the toughness of metals Determine the strength of metals Only (i) and (ii) are correct

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_111 3 2010 Which of the following is NOT a measure of a metal's ductility? Area under the stress-strain diagram measures TOUGHNESS (energy absorbed per unit volume to fracture), not ductility. Ductility is specifically measured by: plastic strain at fracture, percentage elongation (%EL), and percentage reduction in area (%RA). Area under the stress-strain diagram Plastic strain at fracture Percentage elongation Percentage reduction in area All of the above are measures of ductility

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_112 3 2010 Ductile-Brittle Transition Temperature is applicable: The ductile-brittle transition temperature (DBTT) is a characteristic of BCC metals (and some HCP metals). FCC metals remain ductile even at very low temperatures because their {111}<110> slip systems remain active. DBTT does not apply to FCC metals. Only to FCC metals Only to BCC metals For both FCC and BCC metals For all metals For all materials

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_113 3 2010 Which feature is ALWAYS present in a tensile stress-strain diagram of a metal? The fracture point (C) is the ONLY feature always present in a tensile stress-strain diagram — every tensile test must end in fracture. The necking point is NOT always present: brittle metals (e.g. cast iron, ceramics, very hard alloys) fracture cleanly without any visible necking or plastic deformation. A sharply defined yield point is also NOT always present — most metals show a gradual elastic-to-plastic transition requiring the 0.2% offset method. A sharply defined yield point The necking point The fracture point All of the above Only (ii) and (iii)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_114 3 2010 Which statement is correct for a material that fractured before yielding? A material that fractures before yielding is brittle. Brittle materials have little plastic deformation — the area under their stress-strain curve (toughness) is small. So the toughness is low. It's not ductile. Its UTS equals its fracture stress which is ABOVE the elastic region, not below yield. It is a ductile material The toughness of the material will be low Its UTS will be less than its yield stress It does not have elasticity Both (i) and (ii) are correct

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_115 3 2010 Elastic modulus of steel is 200 GPa. Find the strain on a steel rod for a stress of 200 MPa if yield stress is 250 MPa. Since 200 MPa < 250 MPa (yield stress), deformation is elastic. ε = σ/E = 200×10⁶ / 200×10⁹ = 10⁻³ = 0.001. 0.001 0.01 1 100 1000

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_116 3 2010 Impact test can be used to: The Charpy and Izod impact tests measure energy absorbed during fracture. By testing at different temperatures, the ductile-brittle transition temperature can be found (energy drops sharply). Impact tests do NOT measure hardness, electrical resistance, or tensile strength directly. Determine ductile-brittle transition temperature in metals Measure the hardness of brittle materials Determine the electrical resistance of materials Determine the strength of materials None of the above

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_117 3 2010 Which of the following is/are true about ductility and malleability? Ductility is the ability to deform under tensile stress (drawn into wires). Malleability is the ability to deform under compressive stress (hammered into sheets). All ductile materials are also malleable (ductility implies sufficient plastic deformation capacity), but not vice versa — some materials (like lead) are malleable but not ductile. All ductile materials are malleable All malleable materials are ductile Ductility is related to compressive stresses Malleability is related to tensile stresses Only (i) and (iii)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_118 3 2010 Compressive strength of a brick is 300 MN/m². Density is 2000 kg/m³. Maximum theoretical height of a brickwork column without crushing the bottom brick? (g = 10 m/s²) Stress at bottom = ρ × g × h. For max h: σ = ρgh → h = σ/(ρg) = 300×10⁶ / (2000 × 10) = 300×10⁶ / 20,000 = 15,000 m = 15 km. 5 m 10 m 15 m 20 m 15 km

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_119 3 2013 Brittle fracture is more destructive than ductile fracture because: Brittle fracture is characterized by all three: (i) it is catastrophic — sudden and without warning; (ii) cracks propagate at very high speed (close to speed of sound in the material); (iii) once initiated, the crack is self-sustaining — no additional stress is required (Griffith crack theory). All are correct. It is catastrophic Crack propagates at high speed No extra stress needed during crack propagation All of the above Both (i) and (iii)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_120 3 2013 Plastic deformation of a crystalline metal: Plastic deformation is permanent (not recovered), occurs OUTSIDE the Hooke's Law region, and occurs by dislocation movement (slip). It does NOT increase elastic modulus (E depends on atomic bond stiffness, not dislocation density). The correct answer is (iii) only. Is recovered when the applied load is removed Occurs within the Hooke's Law region of a stress-strain curve Occurs due to the movement of dislocations Increases the elastic modulus of a material All of the above are correct

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_121 3 2013 Which statement is correct about toughness? Toughness = area under the stress-strain curve ≈ ability to absorb energy. A material needs BOTH high strength (high stress) AND high ductility (large strain before fracture) to have high toughness. High brittleness = low ductility = less area under curve = less toughness, regardless of strength. High strength + high ductility → more toughness Low strength + low ductility → more toughness High hardness + high strength → more toughness High brittleness + high strength → more toughness High brittleness + low strength → more toughness

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_122 3 2014 During a tensile test, a metal failed before reaching its yield stress. What can be deduced about its deformation up to fracture? If the metal failed before reaching yield stress, it never entered the plastic regime. All deformation up to fracture occurred in the elastic region (below yield). Therefore deformation was completely elastic. Deformation is completely plastic Deformation is completely elastic Deformation is originally elastic then plastic Deformation is originally plastic then elastic Deformation is completely viscoelastic

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_123 3 2014 Both percent elongation and percent reduction in area in a tensile test measure a material's: Both %EL (percentage elongation) and %RA (percentage reduction in area) are quantitative measures of ductility — the ability of a metal to deform plastically before fracture. More elongation and more area reduction = more ductile. Toughness Hardness Ductility Elasticity Robustness

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_124 3 2014 A metal that undergoes plastic deformation ONLY during compression can be called: Malleability is specifically the ability to undergo plastic deformation under compressive stress (e.g., hammering, rolling). A material that can deform plastically in compression but not in tension is malleable (not ductile). Lead is a classic example. Ductile Malleable Brittle Fissile Both (i) and (ii) are correct

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_125 3 2014 The stress at which plastic deformation begins in a metal is known as: The yield strength (or yield stress) is the stress level at which a material transitions from elastic to plastic deformation. Below it, deformation is recoverable; above it, permanent plastic deformation occurs. Proportionality limit Elastic modulus UTS Yield strength Fracture stress

Str. | E A | 310 MPa | 340 MPa | 0.23 | 265 MPa | 210 GPa B | 100 MPa | 120 MPa | 0.40 | 105 MPa | 210 GPa C | 415 MPa | 550 MPa | 0.15 | 500 MPa | 150 GPa D | 700 MPa | 850 MPa | 0.14 | 720 MPa | 210 GPa E | — (fractures before yielding) — | 650 MPa | 350 GPa Using the table (A: YS=310, TS=340, εf=0.23; B: YS=100, TS=120, εf=0.40; C: YS=415, TS=550, εf=0.15; D: YS=700, TS=850, εf=0.14; E: fractures before yielding). Which material has the greatest percent reduction in area? Percent reduction in area is a measure of ductility, which correlates with strain at fracture. Mate

questions.searchText: materials_pu_127 3 2014 📋 Reference table for Q30–Q34 (2014) Material | Yield Str. | Tensile Str. | Strain@Frac | Frac. Str. | E A | 310 MPa | 340 MPa | 0.23 | 265 MPa | 210 GPa B | 100 MPa | 120 MPa | 0.40 | 105 MPa | 210 GPa C | 415 MPa | 550 MPa | 0.15 | 500 MPa | 150 GPa D | 700 MPa | 850 MPa | 0.14 | 720 MPa | 210 GPa E | — (fractures before yielding) — | 650 MPa | 350 GPa From the same table, which material is the strongest? Strength (tensile strength) is the maximum stress a material can withstand. Material D has the highest tensile strength at 850 MPa, making it the strongest. A B C D E

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_128 3 2014 📋 Reference table for Q30–Q34 (2014) Material | Yield Str. | Tensile Str. | Strain@Frac | Frac. Str. | E A | 310 MPa | 340 MPa | 0.23 | 265 MPa | 210 GPa B | 100 MPa | 120 MPa | 0.40 | 105 MPa | 210 GPa C | 415 MPa | 550 MPa | 0.15 | 500 MPa | 150 GPa D | 700 MPa | 850 MPa | 0.14 | 720 MPa | 210 GPa E | — (fractures before yielding) — | 650 MPa | 350 GPa From the same table, which material is the stiffest? Stiffness = Elastic Modulus (E). Material E has the highest elastic modulus (350 GPa), making it the stiffest. A B C D E

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_129 3 2014 📋 Reference table for Q30–Q34 (2014) Material | Yield Str. | Tensile Str. | Strain@Frac | Frac. Str. | E A | 310 MPa | 340 MPa | 0.23 | 265 MPa | 210 GPa B | 100 MPa | 120 MPa | 0.40 | 105 MPa | 210 GPa C | 415 MPa | 550 MPa | 0.15 | 500 MPa | 150 GPa D | 700 MPa | 850 MPa | 0.14 | 720 MPa | 210 GPa E | — (fractures before yielding) — | 650 MPa | 350 GPa From the same table, which material has the greatest ductility? A B C D E

questions.searchText: materials_pu_130 3 2014 📋 Reference table for Q30–Q34 (2014) Material | Yield Str. | Tensile Str. | Strain@Frac | Frac. Str. | E A | 310 MPa | 340 MPa | 0.23 | 265 MPa | 210 GPa B | 100 MPa | 120 MPa | 0.40 | 105 MPa | 210 GPa C | 415 MPa | 550 MPa | 0.15 | 500 MPa | 150 GPa D | 700 MPa | 850 MPa | 0.14 | 720 MPa | 210 GPa E | — (fractures before yielding) — | 650 MPa | 350 GPa From the same table, which material is brittle? Material E fractures before yielding — it fails in the elastic regime with no plastic deformation. This is the definition of brittle behavior. Brittle materials fracture without yielding. A B C D E

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_131 3 2015 Which best describes "Engineering Stress"? Engineering stress is always calculated using the ORIGINAL cross-sectional area (A₀), not the instantaneous area. σ_eng = F/A₀. This is in contrast to true stress which uses the actual (instantaneous) cross-sectional area. Applied load divided by cross-sectional area at fracture Applied load divided by original cross-sectional area Applied load divided by cross-sectional area at UTS Both (i) and (ii) None of the above

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_132 3 2015 The correct definition of yield strength from a tensile test on a ductile material: Yield strength is the stress at the yield point — the point on the stress-strain curve where the material transitions from elastic to plastic behavior. For materials without a clear yield point, a 0.2% offset method is used. It is NOT the maximum load or fracture stress. Stress encountered at the yield point Maximum load divided by final area Stress observed at the fracture point All of the above None of the above

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_133 3 2015 The malleability of a material is the: Malleability is specifically the property of a material that can be mechanically worked, hammered, or rolled into sheets without cracking or breaking. It is the ability to sustain compressive plastic deformation. Option (iii) correctly describes this. Ability to resist deformation and maintain shape Permanent deformation in response to applied forces Property of being worked/hammered/shaped without breaking Ability to absorb energy and deform permanently without fracturing Shattering when an external force is applied

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_134 3 2015 Which stress-strain relationship best describes the behavior of brittle materials such as glass? Brittle materials like glass exhibit linear elastic behavior up to the point of fracture with no plastic region. This is described as "perfectly elastic" — they obey Hooke's law until sudden fracture. There is no yield, no plastic flow. Elastic and perfectly plastic Perfectly plastic Perfectly elastic None of the above Both (ii) and (iii)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_135 3 2015 Hardness of metals is best defined as: For metals, hardness is formally defined as resistance to permanent (plastic) indentation. This is the basis of all standard hardness tests (Brinell, Vickers, Rockwell). Resistance to scratching (Mohs) is used for minerals and ceramics, not metals. Energy absorbed when an object strikes its surface Resistance to bending Resistance to permanent indentation Resistance to scratching Resistance to cutting

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_136 3 2015 Which type(s) of mechanical properties can be obtained from an impact test? The Charpy/Izod impact test measures the energy absorbed during fracture — this is a measure of impact toughness. It does NOT measure hardness (indentation), tensile strength, or elongation. Toughness is the primary property obtained. Hardness Ultimate tensile strength Toughness Percentage elongation at break Both (ii) and (iii)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_137 3 2015 In a tensile test, necking starts at: Necking begins at the Ultimate Tensile Stress (UTS) — the maximum point on the engineering stress-strain curve. Beyond UTS, the local cross-section in the weakest region begins to thin (neck) faster than strain hardening can compensate, leading to localized deformation. Ultimate tensile stress Lower yield stress Upper yield stress Fracture stress None of the above

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_138 3 2015 Flexural strength of a brittle material is commonly determined by: Flexural strength (Modulus of Rupture) of brittle materials is determined by the flexure (three-point or four-point bending) test. Tensile tests are difficult for brittle materials (gripping causes stress concentrations). The three-point bend test is the standard method. Bending test Compression test Flexure test Tensile test Torsion test

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_139 3 2017 Ceramic materials in general have: Ceramics are characterized by: HIGH hardness, HIGH compressive strength, but LOW tensile strength (due to surface flaws acting as stress concentrators), and LOW toughness (brittle — cannot absorb energy by plastic deformation). So both (i) low toughness and (iii) low tensile strength are correct. Low toughness Low hardness Low tensile strength Poor corrosion resistance Both (i) and (iii)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_140 3 2017 A copper rod (300 mm long) is pulled with 275 MPa stress. Find the elongation if deformation is entirely elastic. E = 275 GPa. ε = σ/E = 275×10⁶ / 275×10⁹ = 0.001. ΔL = ε × L = 0.001 × 300 mm = 0.3 mm. 1 mm 300 mm 0.001 mm 0.3 mm 3 mm

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_141 3 2017 📋 Reference setup for Q45–Q46 (2017) A cube made of an isotropic material is subjected to a uniaxial tensile stress σz along the z-axis only (no stress applied in x or y directions). A cube of isotropic material is subjected to tensile stress σz along z-axis only. Which is true about strains in different directions? Tensile stress σz causes: large positive strain εz = σz/E along z (loading direction). Due to Poisson's ratio, lateral contraction: εx = εy = −ν(σz/E). So εx = εy (lateral) are negative and equal, while εz is positive. |εz| > |εx| = |εy|, so εx = εy < εz. εx = εy = εz εx = εy < εz εx = εy > εz εx < εy = εz εx > εy = εz

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_142 3 2017 📋 Reference setup for Q45–Q46 (2017) A cube made of an isotropic material is subjected to a uniaxial tensile stress σz along the z-axis only (no stress applied in x or y directions). With reference to the isotropic cube under σz, which correctly defines Poisson's ratio? Poisson's ratio ν = −(lateral strain)/(axial strain) = −εx/εz. The negative sign is included because lateral strain is opposite in sign to axial strain. εy/εz would also equal −ν, but the standard definition references one lateral direction over axial. εx/εz εx/εy −εx/εz −εx/εy εy/εz

questions.searchText: materials_pu_143 3 2017 📋 Reference setup for Q47–Q48 (2017) Metal rod X: produces elastic strain ε when subjected to tensile stress σ. Metal rod Y: same length L and diameter D as X, but produces elastic strain 2ε under the same stress σ. Rod X gives strain ε under stress σ. Rod Y (same L, D) gives strain 2ε under the same stress σ. What can be deduced? E = σ/ε. For X: Ex = σ/ε. For Y: Ey = σ/2ε = Ex/2. So Ex > Ey. Strength is unrelated to elastic modulus — we cannot compare strength from this data alone. Therefore only (iii) is correct. X is stronger than Y Y is stronger than X Elastic modulus of X > elastic modulus of Y Elastic modulus of Y > elastic modulus of X Both (i) and (iii) are c

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_144 3 2017 📋 Reference setup for Q47–Q48 (2017) Metal rod X: produces elastic strain ε when subjected to tensile stress σ. Metal rod Y: same length L and diameter D as X, but produces elastic strain 2ε under the same stress σ. Rods X (modulus σ/ε, length L) and Y (modulus σ/2ε, length L) are joined end-to-end to form a rod of length 2L. Find the "elastic modulus" of the combined rod. When two rods in series carry the same stress σ, each deforms independently. Rod X extends by εL and Rod Y extends by 2εL. Total extension = 3εL over total length 2L. Effective strain = 3εL/2L = 1.5ε. Therefore E_eff = σ/1.5ε = 2σ/3ε. σ/ε σ/3ε 3σ/ε 2σ/3ε σπD²/12Lε

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_145 3 2017 A metal wire has radius r, length l, and Young's modulus E. Same metal wire with half the radius and double the length: Young's modulus will be: Young's modulus (E = σ/ε) is an intrinsic material property. It depends only on the interatomic bonding, not on the dimensions (radius or length) of the specimen. Therefore E remains the same regardless of changes in geometry. 4E 2E E E/2 E/4

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_146 3 2017 A brass rod (diameter 10 mm) is loaded with tensile stress along its axis. Find the load to produce a 0.0025 mm change in diameter. E = 97 GPa, ν = 0.34. Lateral strain = ΔD/D = −0.0025/10 = −2.5×10⁻⁴. Axial strain = −(lateral strain)/ν = 2.5×10⁻⁴/0.34 = 7.35×10⁻⁴. Axial stress σ = E×ε = 97×10⁹ × 7.35×10⁻⁴ = 71.3 MPa. Area = π(0.005)² = 7.854×10⁻⁵ m². F = σA = 71.3×10⁶ × 7.854×10⁻⁵ ≈ 5600 N. 280 N 560 N 2800 N 5600 N None of the above

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_147 3 2017 Five specimens of a metal rod are tensile tested. Which property will most likely have the LEAST standard deviation? Elastic modulus (Young's modulus) is determined by atomic bonding forces, which are consistent throughout the material and not affected by microstructural variations, surface defects, or test setup differences. UTS, yield strength, elongation, and fracture strength all vary with microstructure, heat treatment history, and surface condition. Ultimate tensile strength Yield strength Percentage elongation Fracture strength Elastic modulus

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_148 3 2018 Two metals A and B tested for Vickers hardness: depth of indentation in B was double that in A. What can be deduced about their Vickers hardness values? Vickers hardness is calculated from the surface area of the indentation: HV ∝ F/d² (where d is diagonal length). If the depth of indentation is doubled, the diagonal is approximately doubled (geometric relationship). Then HV ∝ 1/d². If dB = 2dA: HVA/HVB = dB²/dA² = (2dA)²/dA² = 4. So HVA = 4 × HVB. HV of A is double that of B HV of B is double that of A HV of A is four times that of B HV of B is four times that of A Both metals have equal hardness

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_149 3 2018 Metal A has higher elastic modulus than metal B. After applying tensile stresses, longitudinal strains are EQUAL. What can be deduced about the stresses? From Hooke's Law: σ = E × ε. If εA = εB and EA > EB, then σA = EA × ε > EB × ε = σB. The stress on rod A must be greater than that on rod B. Dimensions (diameter, length) do not affect elastic stress-strain relationship. Stress on A > stress on B Stress on B > stress on A Stresses are the same on both Cannot find without knowing diameters Cannot find without knowing lengths

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_150 3 2018 Percentage elongation in a tensile test measures the ________ of a metal. Percentage elongation (%EL) = (Lf − L₀)/L₀ × 100. It measures how much a metal can stretch plastically before fracture, which is a direct measure of ductility. A higher %EL = more ductile. Strength Elasticity Toughness Ductility Both (iii) and (iv)

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_151 3 2018 What is the maximum possible value of Poisson's ratio? Theoretical thermodynamics constrains Poisson's ratio: −1 ≤ ν ≤ 0.5. The upper limit of 0.5 corresponds to incompressible materials (no volume change during deformation). Rubber approaches this limit (ν ≈ 0.5). Most metals have ν ≈ 0.25–0.35. 0.1 0.33 0.5 1 Infinity

This corresponds to ν ≈ 0.5 (the theoretical maximum). Metals typically have ν ≈ 0.25–0.35. Rubber's high Poisson's ratio explains why it bulges significantly when compressed. 0.1 0.33 0.5 1 Infinity

True stress > engineering stress; true strain < engineering strain True stress > engineering stress; true strain = engineering strain True stress > engineering stress; true strain > engineering strain True stress < engineering stress; true strain < engineering strain True stress < engineering stress; true strain > engineering strain

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_154 3 2018 A material with greater toughness will always have: Toughness = area under the stress-strain curve. In principle, high toughness can be achieved by high strength with moderate ductility OR moderate strength with high ductility. There is no single combination that "always" produces high toughness — it depends on the specific material and the trade-offs involved. Therefore it cannot be generalized. High strength and high ductility High strength and low ductility Low strength and high ductility Low strength and low ductility It cannot be generalized

questions.searchText: materials_pu_155 3 2018 A ceramic tile: thickness x, width 5x, length 10x, span = 80% of length. Force at fracture = F. Find flexural strength. MOR = 3PL/(2bd²) P=F, L=0.8×10x=8x, b=5x, d=x. MOR = 3PL/(2bd²) = 3F(8x)/(2×5x×x²) = 24Fx/(10x³) = 2.4Fx/x³ = 2.4F/x² = 2.4Fx⁻². 3Fx⁻² 3Fx² 2.4Fx² 2.4Fx⁻² Fx

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_156 3 2018 During tensile test of metals after yielding, the gradient of stress-strain curve decreases up to necking. This indicates: The decreasing slope (strain hardening rate dσ/dε decreasing) after yield indicates the material is being work hardened — dislocations multiply and impede each other, requiring increasing stress for further deformation, but at a decreasing rate. Elastic modulus does NOT change during plastic deformation. The material is becoming tougher The material is becoming weaker The material is being work hardened The elastic modulus is decreasing Both (iii) and (iv) are correct

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_157 3 2010 In Rockwell hardness testing, both Rockwell-A and Rockwell-C scales use the same indenter (diamond cone). The loads applied in Rockwell-A and Rockwell-C are 60 kg and 150 kg respectively. If a given metal sample is tested on both scales, which of the following would be correct? In Rockwell testing, hardness is measured by depth of indentation — a smaller indentation means a HIGHER Rockwell number. Since Rockwell-C uses a heavier load (150 kg vs 60 kg), it drives the indenter deeper, giving a smaller Rockwell number. Therefore Rockwell-A will always give a higher numerical value than Rockwell-C for the same material. Hardness values will be the same in both cases Rockw

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_158 3 2010 Which of the following statements is/are correct about compression testing? All three are correct. Brittle materials (ceramics, concrete) are far easier to test in compression as they survive gripping better. Ductile metals barrel outward (no neck) in compression. And for ductile metals, the von Mises yield criterion means compressive and tensile yield strengths are approximately equal. Widely used for testing brittle materials Ductile materials show no necking during the test Failure stress of a ductile material in compression is almost the same as in a tensile test All of the above are correct Only (i) and (iii) are correct

Fiberglass (glass fibre-reinforced polymer composite) is engineered so that the glass fibres arrest crack propagation through the matrix, giving it by far the highest fracture toughness of these options. questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_161 3 2013 Hardness of brittle materials is defined as: In engineering materials science, hardness of all solid materials — brittle or ductile — is formally defined as the resistance to localised permanent deformation (indentation). Scratch hardness (Mohs) is used in mineralogy but is not the standard engineering definition. Resistance to indentation Resistance to scratching Resistance to forging Resistance to elongation Resistance to failure under an impact load

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_162 3 2013 Which of the following parameters CANNOT be determined from a uniaxial tensile stress-strain diagram? The Modulus of Rupture (MOR / flexural strength) is obtained from a separate 3-point or 4-point bending test, not from a uniaxial tensile test. All other properties listed — UTS, yield point, Young's modulus, and elongation at fracture — are directly read from the tensile stress-strain curve. Tensile strength Yield point Young's modulus Modulus of Rupture Elongation

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_163 3 2013 The directional dependence of physical properties of a material is termed as: Anisotropy is the property whereby a material exhibits different mechanical or physical properties depending on the direction of measurement. Isotropy is the opposite — properties are the same in all directions. Single crystals and composites are classic examples of anisotropic materials. Allotropy Crystallinity Anisotropy Amorphous Isotropy

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_164 3 2013 Steel is an alloy that is: Carbon atoms (atomic radius ~0.077 nm) are much smaller than iron atoms (0.126 nm). Rather than replacing iron atoms on lattice sites (substitutional), they squeeze into the tetrahedral or octahedral void spaces (interstices) between iron atoms — forming an interstitial solid solution. A substitutional solid solution of carbon in iron An interstitial solid solution of carbon in iron Formed by rapid cooling of molten iron to room temperature A self-interstitial of iron in the BCC lattice None of the above

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_165 3 2013 Compressive strength of a brick is 300 MPa and its density is 2000 kg/m³. To what theoretical maximum height can a brickwork column be constructed without crushing the bottom-most brick? Take g = 10 m/s². The stress at the base equals ρgh. Setting this equal to the compressive strength: 300 × 10⁶ = 2000 × 10 × h. Solving: h = 300 × 10⁶ / 20000 = 15,000 m = 15 km. 10 m 15 m 10 km 15 km 20 km

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_166 3 2013 If the bricks in the previous question are dampened by rain, what will be the maximum possible height of this brickwork column? Assume the strength is unaffected and the volume of water absorbed is 30% of brick volume (density of water = 1000 kg/m³). The effective density increases: ρ_new = 2000 + (0.30 × 1000) = 2300 kg/m³. New max height: h = 300 × 10⁶ / (2300 × 10) = 13,043 m ≈ 13 km. The added water mass lowers the safe column height. 10 m 10 km 13 km 15 km More data required

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_167 3 2014 Which of the following is a property of a material that CANNOT be categorised as a mechanical property? Neutron absorption is a nuclear property that describes how the atomic nucleus interacts with neutron radiation — it has nothing to do with the response to macroscopic mechanical forces. All other options (hardness, strength, toughness, shock absorption) describe how a material responds to applied forces or impacts. Hardness Strength Toughness Neutron absorption Shock absorption

questions.subject: materials
questions.searchText: materials_pu_175 3 2018 For rubber, which of the following is true about Tangent modulus vs Secant modulus at a given stress? Rubber exhibits a highly non-linear S-shaped stress-strain curve. In the initial soft region the slope is gentle (low tangent modulus), then it stiffens significantly at large strains (high tangent modulus). At low stress, the secant slope (average from origin) can exceed the tangent slope; at high stress during stiffening, the tangent exceeds the secant. Therefore neither is consistently greater — it depends on where you are on the curve. Tangent modulus equals Secant modulus Tangent modulus is always greater than Secant modulus Tangent modulus is always less than Se

questions.searchText: materials_pu_186 4 2017 The Fermi level of an impure silicon semiconductor material is found to be 0.25 eV above the valence-band edge at 300K. Structural materials provide passive mechanical support. Functional materials are "active" and are categorized by what they do in response to an environment—such as optical, magnetic, or electronic responses. Functional materials are exclusively organic polymers, whereas structural materials are strictly inorganic metals or ceramics. Functional materials must possess a completely amorphous atomic structure, whereas structural materials must be highly crystalline. Functional materials are selected based on their dynamic response to external s
